_doc: Synthetic K-8 Social Studies activity ledger for the feedback-integrity harness. The setting is a FICTIONAL world (invented peoples, places, and goods) authored from scratch for this public demo. The fiction is load-bearing, not decoration: it is what makes the blind-solver a valid measurement. If the items used real-world facts (rivers, the Constitution, supply and demand), a model could 'solve' them from prior knowledge and we would be measuring its social-studies knowledge, not whether the feedback leaked the answer. With invented content the ONLY way to name an item's arbitrary correct answer is if the wrong-answer feedback gave it away. This is the same discipline expert-parity uses with its fictional insurer. Each item is a 4-option MCQ: one option has correct:true (the key); each WRONG option carries the wrong-answer feedback a student would see. Flaw labels are NOT in this file; they live in golden/findings.json. Nothing here is sourced from, seeded by, or de-identified from any real client bank (firewall: synthetic-only, NEUTRAL).
world: A fictional river-and-mountain region. Peoples: the Korin, the Tola, the Mott, the Vale folk, the Suun, the Red Cliffs people. Places: Lake Onu, the Greywood, the Ashen Flats, Mount Sel, Old Harbor, the Red Cliffs. Goods: amber, reed-salt, blackwool, river-glass. Institutions: the Council of Nine, the Charter of Onu.

items.stem: Why did the Korin people first build their village beside Lake Onu?

options.text: The calm lake gave them fish to eat and clean water all year.
Because Mount Sel was an easy hill to climb in the winter.
options.feedback: Mount Sel was a steep, icy peak, not a place where a village could grow.

options.text: Because the Greywood held the tallest trees in all the land.
options.feedback: The Greywood gave shade, but it was dark and easy to get lost in.

options.text: Because the Ashen Flats were a good place to race carts.
options.feedback: The Ashen Flats were dusty and bare, with little there for daily life.

items.stem: What was the main duty of the Korin Council of Nine?

options.text: To settle disputes between families and keep the peace.
To lead the long hunts deep into the Greywood.
options.feedback: Not quite. The Council of Nine's main duty was to settle disputes between families and keep the peace.

options.text: To paint the trading boats before the spring market.
options.feedback: Decorating the boats was the work of the craft guild, not the council.

options.text: To choose which songs were sung at the festivals.
options.feedback: Festival songs were chosen by the singers themselves, not by any ruling group.

items.stem: Why did the Tola people move to the edge of the Ashen Flats?

options.text: To dig the rare blue salt buried under the pale ground.
Because the Flats had the richest soil for tall crops.
options.feedback: The Ashen Flats were dry and bare, and almost nothing would grow there.

options.text: Because the Mott chased them there during a long war.
options.feedback: Remember, the Tola came to this dusty place for one reason: the valuable salt that could be dug out from below.

options.text: Because the Flats were a holy place for their gods.
options.feedback: The Tola built no temples on the Flats; their move was about work, not worship.

items.stem: What was the job of the tollkeeper at Old Harbor?

options.text: To collect a fee from each ship that entered the harbor.
To catch enough fish to feed the whole town.
options.feedback: Feeding the town was the fishers' work, not the keeper of the gate.

options.text: To ring the great bell at sunrise each day.
options.feedback: Remember, the saltrunner carried heavy sacks of reed-salt up the cliff road.

options.text: To build the new trading boats each season.
options.feedback: Boat-building was done in the yards, a separate trade from the harbor gate.

items.stem: What did the Mott people trade most often at the spring market?

options.text: Soft blackwool sheared from their mountain sheep.
Bright amber beads gathered from the river.
options.feedback: Bright amber beads gathered from the river.

options.text: Heavy iron tools made in the lowland forges.
options.feedback: The lowland forges made iron tools, but the smiths traded those, not the Mott.

options.text: Painted clay pots shaped by the Old Harbor potters.
options.feedback: Clay pots came from the Old Harbor potters, a different people than the Mott.

items.stem: Why did the Vale folk build a tall stone wall around their town?

options.text: To protect themselves from raiders coming over the hills.
To keep their sheep from wandering off into the valley.
options.feedback: Holding back the spring floods was the job of the river dam, not a wall.

options.text: To show the other towns how wealthy they had become.
options.feedback: The wall was about safety, not about showing off the town's riches.

options.text: To hold back the river floods every spring.
options.feedback: The spring floods were stopped by a dam downstream, not by the town wall.

items.stem: What was the Charter of Onu?

options.text: It was the written agreement that set down the laws of the nine villages, named who would sit on the Council, and listed the rights that every family was promised.
It was a kind of fishing net.
options.feedback: A fishing net is only a tool, which this clearly is not.

options.text: It was a festival held in summer.
options.feedback: This was not a yearly celebration that people gathered for.

options.text: It was a song about the lake.
options.feedback: This was not a tune that anyone stood up to sing.

items.stem: Why did the Vale folk send scouts into the Greywood each autumn?

options.text: To gather the ripe amber-nuts before the frost arrived.
To cut down the oldest trees to build new boats.
options.feedback: Boat wood was taken from the river groves, not from deep in the Greywood.

options.text: To hunt the great elk for a midwinter feast.
options.feedback: The autumn scouts went to store up food, not for a single feast hunt.

options.text: To meet the traders coming from the Red Cliffs.
options.feedback: Traders were always met at Old Harbor, never deep inside the woods.

items.stem: What did the red banner of the Red Cliffs people stand for?

options.text: It honored the iron miners who first dug into the cliffs.
It marked the way to the summer trading market.
options.feedback: The market paths were marked with white posts, not with a banner.

options.text: It warned the boats of storms coming off the sea.
options.feedback: Storm warnings were given by ringing the harbor bell, not by a flag.

options.text: It showed which family would rule the cliffs that year.
options.feedback: No single family ruled the cliffs; the people shared the work.

items.stem: How did the Suun people choose who would lead them each year?

options.text: Each household placed a stone, and the one with the most stones led.
The oldest person in the village always became the leader.
options.feedback: Actually, each household placed a stone, and whoever had the most stones became the leader.

options.text: The leader was chosen for them by the Council of Nine.
options.feedback: The Council led the Korin, not the Suun, who had their own way.

options.text: Whoever won the autumn foot-race led the people.
options.feedback: The race was run for sport and pride, not for picking a leader.

items.stem: What happened at the Tola people's yearly Salt Festival?

options.text: They shared the season's salt and gave thanks for a good dig.
They raced their boats all the way across Lake Onu.
options.feedback: Boat races belonged to the Korin's water games, not to the Salt Festival.

options.text: They lit rows of lanterns along the high cliff path.
options.feedback: Remember, astronauts must wear special suits in order to breathe out in space.

options.text: They held a great market for traders from every town.
options.feedback: Trading happened at the spring market, a separate event from this festival.

items.stem: Why did the Mott people build their homes high on the mountain?

options.text: The high slopes kept their sheep safe from the valley wolves.
They wanted to live closer to the deep winter snow.
options.feedback: No one built up high just to sit nearer to the cold snow.

options.text: The mountaintop was the only flat ground for miles.
options.feedback: The peaks were steep and uneven, and not flat at all.

options.text: They were ordered to move there by the Council of Nine.
options.feedback: The Mott governed themselves and took no orders from the Korin council.

items.stem: Why did the people of Old Harbor light a fire on the point each night?

options.text: To guide their fishing boats safely back into the harbor.
To cook the whole day's catch for the town to share.
options.feedback: Scaring raiders away from the coast was the wall guards' job, not a harbor fire.

options.text: To frighten away the raiders who came from the sea.
options.feedback: Raiders were kept off by the tall stone wall, not by a single fire.

options.text: To celebrate the very end of the fishing season.
options.feedback: The fire on the point was lit all year, not only when the season ended.

items.stem: Why did traders take the cliff road between Old Harbor and the Red Cliffs?

options.text: It was the only path that wagons could climb with heavy loads.
It was the shortest way by a full half a day.
options.feedback: The cliff road was actually the long way around, not the shortest.

options.text: It passed the best inns for stopping to rest.
options.feedback: There were no inns on the bare cliff road; traders camped in the open.

options.text: It was the one road free of any tolls or fees.
options.feedback: Tolls were still collected on the cliff road, the same as everywhere else.
